threats.title: Logic Error in Certificate Date Tag Validation Allows Malformed Certificates
threats.hypothesis_description: The `notBefore` date tag validation in `decodeCert` contains a logical flaw that rejects valid GeneralizedTime tags while accepting arbitrary invalid tags, potentially allowing certificates with malformed or spoofed validity dates to bypass verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` is logically equivalent to `notBeforeTag == 0x18`. This means it incorrectly rejects `0x18` (GeneralizedTime) and accepts any other non-`0x17` tag (e.g., `0x05`). This allows an attacker to submit a certificate with a malformed date tag that `X509DateUtils` might parse as perpetually valid.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in tag validation: (notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)

preconditions: Attacker submits a quote with a custom PCK certificate chain
The certificate uses an invalid ASN.1 tag for the notBefore field

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Flawed boolean logic using && instead of || or != instead of ==
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rejects valid 0x18 tags and accepts invalid tags like 0x05
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No secondary validation of the parsed timestamp bounds

analysis_questions: How does X509DateUtils handle the bytes following an invalid tag?
Can a malformed date string result in a timestamp of 0 or a very large number?
Does the signature verification still pass if the date tag is modified?
validation_requirements: Construct a DER certificate with an invalid notBefore tag
Trace the execution through decodeCert and X509DateUtils
Verify if the modified certificate still passes the signature check (it shouldn't if the signature covers the TBSCertificate, but needs confirmation)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker modifies the ASN.1 tag of the notBefore field in a revoked or expired certificate to bypass date checks, potentially allowing an invalid SGX quote to be accepted.
prerequisites: Ability to forge or modify a certificate such that its signature remains valid, or exploit a parsing differential

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Compromise of the L2 proving system if invalid SGX quotes are accepted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users relying on SGX proofs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix the parsing logic

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for certificates with GeneralizedTime (0x18) and invalid date tags

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect on-chain without parsing the raw bytes

threats.title: Cross-Chain and Cross-Instance Signature Replay in TimelockTokenPool
threats.hypothesis_description: The `withdraw(address,bytes)` function verifies an ECDSA signature that lacks a domain separator (chain ID, contract address) and a nonce, allowing signatures to be replayed across different chains or instances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The signed hash is simply `keccak256(abi.encodePacked("Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ", _to))`. If a recipient has grants on multiple chains (e.g., L1 and L2) or in multiple pool instances, an attacker can capture a signature used on one chain and replay it on another, forcing the recipient to withdraw tokens to the `_to` address on a chain where they might not control that address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ECDSA.recover used with a hash lacking chainid, address(this), and nonce

preconditions: Recipient has a grant in multiple TimelockTokenPool instances or across chains
Recipient signs a withdrawal transaction for one instance
The `_to` address is a contract or address the recipient controls on one chain but not the other

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Signature is globally valid for any TimelockTokenPool contract on any chain
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonce, chainid, or contract address in the signed message

analysis_questions: Are there multiple instances of TimelockTokenPool deployed?
Is the contract deployed on both L1 and L2?
Can a replay cause actual loss of funds (e.g., if `_to` is a multisig deployed at different addresses on different chains)?
validation_requirements: Check deployment scripts for multiple instances
Test cross-chain replay in a local fork environment

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user signs a withdrawal to their L2 smart contract wallet. An attacker replays this signature on L1. The L1 tokens are sent to the L2 smart contract wallet address on L1, where the user has no control, resulting in lost tokens.
prerequisites: User must sign at least one withdrawal transaction
Attacker must monitor mempool or blockchain for the signature

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of user's vested tokens on the replayed chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user utilizing the delegated withdrawal feature
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Tokens are lost if the `_to` address is inaccessible on the target chain

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed anytime after the original signature is broadcast
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a withdrawal triggered by a third party

threats.title: Root Certificate Expiration and Revocation Bypass in SGX Attestation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_verifyCertChain` function breaks its validation loop immediately upon finding the root CA public key, skipping the expiration and signature validation for the root certificate itself.
threats.investigation_rationale: When iterating through the certificate chain, if `issuerPubKeyHash == ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH`, the loop sets `certChainCanBeTrusted = true` and breaks. For a standard 3-cert chain, this happens when checking the intermediate cert (i=1). As a result, the loop never reaches i=2, meaning the root certificate's `notBefore` and `notAfter` dates are never checked. An attacker could use an expired root certificate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Early loop break skips validation of the final element in the chain

preconditions: Attacker submits a quote with an expired root certificate
The root certificate's public key matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Early break before validating the trust anchor's own metadata
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Root certificate expiry dates are ignored
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check on certs[n-1].notAfter before returning true

analysis_questions: Does the root certificate contain critical revocation or policy extensions that are being skipped?
Can an expired root certificate be used to validate intermediate certificates that were issued after the root expired?
validation_requirements: Construct a cert chain with an expired root certificate

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Intel's root CA expires and is replaced, an attacker could continue using the old, expired root CA certificate to validate old intermediate certificates, potentially bypassing new security policies or revocations tied to the new root.
prerequisites: Availability of an expired root CA certificate that matches the hardcoded hash

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential acceptance of invalid SGX proofs

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests with expired root certificates

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect without parsing the submitted quote

threats.title: Array Out-of-Bounds DoS in PCK Certificate TCB Parsing
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_findTcb` function in `PEMCertChainLib` iterates one time too many for the `cpusvns` array. If the `PCESVN_OID` is not the last element in the ASN.1 sequence, the function will attempt to write to an out-of-bounds array index, causing a revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `cpusvns` array is initialized with length `SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE` (16). The loop iterates `SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1` (17) times. If `PCESVN_OID` is encountered, it sets `pcesvn` and skips the array write. However, if `PCESVN_OID` is not the 17th element, the 17th iteration will hit the `else` block and execute `cpusvns[16] = cpusvn`, which reverts in Solidity 0.8.x. This makes the parser fragile to valid variations in certificate extension ordering.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop iterates N+1 times over an array of size N, relying on an if-branch to avoid the Nth index write

preconditions: A PCK certificate is submitted where the PCESVN extension is not the last element in the TCB sequence

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cpusvns[i] where i can be 16, but array length is 16
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check before writing to cpusvns[i]

analysis_questions: Does Intel guarantee that PCESVN is always the last element in the sequence?
Can an attacker craft a valid certificate with a different ordering?
validation_requirements: Construct a DER sequence where PCESVN is the first element
Run `_findTcb` and verify it reverts with an out-of-bounds error

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Intel issues certificates with a different extension order, all SGX attestation verifications will revert, causing a complete halt of the L2 proving system.
prerequisites: Intel changing certificate format, or an attacker finding a way to reorder extensions without invalidating the signature

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service for the proving system, halting the rollup

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests with varying SVN extension orderings

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as reverted transactions with Panic(0x32) (Array out of bounds)

threats.title: Precision Loss in TimelockTokenPool Allows Value Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: The `costToWithdraw` calculation in `TimelockTokenPool` divides by `1e18` before multiplying by `costPerToken`, leading to truncation. A user can strategically leave a fractional amount (e.g., 0.999e18) unwithdrawn to receive those tokens for free.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula `costToWithdraw = (amountUnlocked / 1e18) * costPerToken - costPaid` truncates the fractional part of `amountUnlocked`. If a user withdraws `X.999e18` tokens, they only pay for `X` tokens. While subsequent withdrawals will catch up on the cost, the user can simply choose to never withdraw the final fractional amount, effectively gaining up to `0.999 * costPerToken` value for free per grant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division before multiplication causing precision loss

preconditions: User has a grant with a non-zero `costPerToken`
User's unlocked amount has a fractional component close to 1e18

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Users pay less costToken than mathematically required for the exact amount of taikoToken withdrawn

analysis_questions: Is the value extracted significant enough to warrant exploitation?
Does the protocol intend for `costPerToken` to apply strictly to whole tokens only?
validation_requirements: Simulate a grant and withdraw X.999e18 tokens
Verify the cost paid is exactly X * costPerToken
Verify the user retains the 0.999e18 tokens without paying for them

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user monitors their vesting schedule and withdraws exactly when their unlocked amount is X.999e18. They pay for X tokens but receive X.999e18. They never withdraw the remaining 0.001e18, keeping the unpaid fractional tokens.
prerequisites: User must be a grant recipient

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Minor loss of costToken revenue for the protocol per user

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for fractional token withdrawals with costPerToken

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires specific timing to match the vesting schedule
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a mismatch between tokens withdrawn and cost paid

threats.title: Division by Zero DoS in ERC20Airdrop2 if Withdrawal Window is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: If `ERC20Airdrop2` is initialized with a `_withdrawalWindow` of 0, the `getBalance` function will revert due to a division by zero, permanently locking all airdropped tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `init` function sets `withdrawalWindow = _withdrawalWindow` without validating that it is greater than 0. The `getBalance` function calculates `timeBasedAllowance` by dividing by `withdrawalWindow`. If it is 0, any call to `withdraw` or `getBalance` will revert. This is a critical configuration dependency that can brick the contract.
deployment_assumptions: The deployer accidentally or intentionally sets `_withdrawalWindow` to 0 during initialization
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script provided for ERC20Airdrop2 to verify the intended parameter value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by a configurable state variable without a zero check

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Unvalidated initialization parameter used as a divisor

analysis_questions: Is there any off-chain validation preventing a 0 value?
Can the contract be upgraded to fix this if it happens?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call getBalance and verify it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A deployer misconfigures the contract. Users successfully claim their airdrop, but when they attempt to withdraw, the transaction reverts, locking their funds permanently.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete lock of all airdropped tokens in the vault
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade (contract is UUPS upgradeable, so recovery is possible)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for initialization with 0 withdrawal window

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as reverted withdrawal transactions

threats.title: Gas Exhaustion DoS in SigVerifyLib via assert()
threats.hypothesis_description: The `verifyES256Signature` function uses `assert(success)` after a `staticcall` to an external verifier contract. If the verifier reverts, the `assert` consumes all remaining transaction gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Solidity, `assert(false)` compiles to the `INVALID` opcode (0xfe), which consumes all gas provided to the transaction. If the `ES256VERIFIER` contract has a bug, is not deployed, or intentionally reverts on specific malformed inputs, an attacker can submit a quote that triggers this revert, causing the relayer or user to lose the maximum possible gas.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Use of assert() on external call success

preconditions: The ES256VERIFIER contract reverts during the staticcall

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does the P256Verifier contract revert?
Can an attacker craft a signature that causes the verifier to revert instead of returning false?
validation_requirements: Analyze the P256Verifier implementation for revert conditions
Simulate a call that causes a revert and verify gas consumption

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker submits a malformed SGX quote with a signature specifically crafted to cause the P256Verifier to revert. The relayer processing the transaction loses a significant amount of gas, creating a griefing vector.
prerequisites: Ability to trigger a revert in the external verifier contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gas loss for relayers or users submitting attestations

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for verifier revert scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Verify if all gas is consumed
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as transactions reverting with out-of-gas or Panic(0x01)

threats.title: CrossChainOwned Reentrancy Sequence Gap
threats.hypothesis_description: The `onMessageInvocation` function increments `nextTxId` after executing a low-level call to `address(this)`. If the executed function can trigger another bridge message processing, it could cause sequence gaps or reuse of `nextTxId`.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function explicitly omits `nonReentrant` because it expects to be re-entered by the `address(this).call(txdata)`. However, because `nextTxId++` happens after the call, any re-entrant execution of `onMessageInvocation` during the call would see the old `nextTxId`. If the inner call reverts but the outer call succeeds (which is prevented by `if (!success) revert`), or if the inner call succeeds and increments `nextTxId`, the outer call will increment it again, potentially skipping a sequence number and permanently breaking the cross-chain sequence.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State update (nextTxId++) after external/low-level call without reentrancy guard

preconditions: The executed `txdata` must be able to trigger another call to `onMessageInvocation` via the bridge

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nextTxId is incremented after the call
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: nextTxId should be incremented before the call

analysis_questions: Is there any function in the contract that can trigger the bridge to deliver another message synchronously?
What happens if `txdata` is a call to `onMessageInvocation` itself? (It would fail the `onlyFromNamed('bridge')` check unless the bridge itself can be re-entered)
validation_requirements: Analyze the Bridge contract implementation to see if synchronous multi-message delivery is possible
Attempt to construct a re-entrant payload

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker (or compromised owner) crafts a payload that causes a re-entrant call to `onMessageInvocation`. The inner call succeeds and increments `nextTxId`. The outer call then succeeds and increments `nextTxId` again. The sequence skips a number, permanently DoSing future cross-chain owner commands because the source chain expects the skipped number.
prerequisites: Ability to craft a re-entrant payload through the bridge

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade via a different mechanism (if any exists)

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a skipped nextTxId
